content.problemStatement:
Little kids, Jack and Evan like playing their favorite game Glass-and-Stone. Today they want to play something new and came across Twitter on their father's laptop.


They saw it for the first time but were already getting bored to see a bunch of sentences having at most 140 characters each. The only thing they liked to play with it is, closing and opening tweets.


There are **N** tweets on the page and each tweet can be opened by clicking on it, to see some statistics related to that tweet. Initially all the tweets are closed. Clicking on an open tweet closes it and clicking on a closed tweet opens it. There is also a button to close all the open tweets. Given a sequence of **K** clicks by Jack, Evan has to guess the total number of open tweets just after each click. Please help Evan in this game.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two integers N and K (1 ≤ N ≤ 1000, 1 ≤ K ≤ 1000) – the number of tweets and the number of click operations. Each of the next K lines contains either "CLICK X" (1 ≤ X ≤ N) or "CLOSEALL". Initially all tweets are closed for every test case.
content.outputFormat: For each click operation, output a line containing the number of open tweets immediately after that operation. The outputs of all test cases are printed sequentially, each on its own line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case with three tweets. After each click the number of open tweets evolves as described in the statement.
First test case is the same as sample_1. The second test case toggles tweet 1 twice (opening then closing), opens tweet 2, then closes all.
Demonstrates that CLOSEALL works even when no tweets are open and that toggling the same tweet twice returns it to closed.

solution.approach:
Maintain a boolean array (or integer 0/1) of size N indicating whether each tweet is open. Keep a counter of currently open tweets. For each command:
- If it is "CLICK X", toggle the state of tweet X and adjust the counter (+1 if it becomes open, -1 if it becomes closed).
- If it is "CLOSEALL", reset the array to all closed and set the counter to 0.
After processing the command, output the counter. The algorithm runs in O(K) per test case and uses O(N) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * (N + K)) – each test case may reset the whole array on a CLOSEALL (O(N)), but the total work is bounded by the sum of K and occasional O(N) resets, well within limits.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) – the array storing the state of each tweet.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().splitlines()
    if not data:
        return
    idx = 0
    T = int(data[idx]); idx += 1
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N, K = map(int, data[idx].split()); idx += 1
        tweets = [0] * N
        open_cnt = 0
        for _ in range(K):
            parts = data[idx].split(); idx += 1
            if parts[0] == 'CLICK':
                x = int(parts[1]) - 1
                if tweets[x] == 0:
                    tweets[x] = 1
                    open_cnt += 1
                else:
                    tweets[x] = 0
                    open_cnt -= 1
            else:  # CLOSEALL
                tweets = [0] * N
                open_cnt = 0
            out_lines.append(str(open_cnt))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
